don't you think that `OmnisciOnPython` is a more appropriate name for this backend?

Speaking formally, a backend is the content of partitions - for PandasOn* the partitions contain pandas DataFrames, for PyarrowOnRay, partitions contain Pyarrow tables, and so on. An engine is some kind of partition manager - for *OnRay the ray manages partitions, for *OnPython it's only a python role to do this and so on.

In the case of Omnisci backend pseudo-partitions contain Omnisci server, so the proper naming would be OmnisciOn*. Previously the partitions manager appears to be Ray, when we'll remove it, pseudo-partitions would be managed by the Python code only, so it seems that the Python becomes the new engine, and so the resulted name for the "new" backend should be `OmnisciOnPython`. What do you think?

Yeah, this deserves a comment explaining it more -- the first `wrap_streaming_content`'s utility is more evident, but this one isn't.

The complexity is that `HoneyDBWrapper` only works for database queries run inside its `with contextlib.ExitStack()`.  Without this, the queries are run after the `response` is returned, well after the `contextlib.ExitStack()` has cleaned up the contexts that do the tracing.

So this `wrap_streaming_content` sets up a second context that runs later, in the context of when the generator is being called, that provides that same context again.  The first hunk above makes the request not end until the streaming is done; this one ensures that DB queries that happen while in that streaming section are recorded properly.

It is ordering by ``-updated`` which is the ``updated`` field of the latesthost, *NOT* of the actual host.
This would be fine off of a fresh database since the "updated" field would continuously get updated.

However, because we are not supplying the "created" and "updated" timestamps when running the SQL migration, they are set to "now" instead of "whenever it was for that host" so sorting by date on a database that has run migrations leads to results that are not completely wrong but a bit weird.

And for these applications we cannot use KeyRefs as the idea of key refs is that they keys are stored in secure storage and only the references are used in the stack. This is a good question. I have test many cases:
1. If the protobuf is built in this project, the generated `.cmake` files will expose the dependency on ABSL libs, so it not necessary to use this `protobuf_ABSL_USED_TARGETS`, which means it's OK to be empty. 
2. `("${Protobuf_VERSION}" < "4.22.0")` there is no such dependency.
3. When protobuf is pre-installed and  when `("${Protobuf_VERSION}" >= "4.22.0")`, there could be no such generated `.cmake` files in pre-installed package and `find_package` only returns the protobuf libs only. In this case, we need to use this protobuf_ABSL_USED_TARGETS. Otherewise, there will be link errors.

Finnally, the conclusion is that `protobuf_ABSL_USED_TARGETS` is needed when protobuf is pre-installed and  when `("${Protobuf_VERSION}" VERSION_GREATER_EQUAL "4.22.0")`.

I searched a bit and found that there seems to be a solution mentioned in [RFC-7050 section 3](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7050#section-3) about this:

> A node MUST look through all of the received AAAA resource records to
>    collect one or more Pref64::/n.  The Pref64::/n list might include
>    the Well-Known Prefix 64:ff9b::/96 [[RFC6052](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6052)] or one or more Network-
>    Specific Prefixes.  In the case of NSPs, the node SHALL determine the
>    used address format by searching the received IPv6 addresses for the
>    WKN's well-known IPv4 addresses.  The node SHALL assume the well-
>    known IPv4 addresses might be found at the locations specified by
>    [[RFC6052], Section 2.2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6052#section-2.2).  **The node MUST check on octet boundaries to
>    ensure a 32-bit well-known IPv4 address value is present only once in
>    an IPv6 address.  In case another instance of the value is found
>    inside the IPv6 address, the node SHALL repeat the search with the
>    other well-known IPv4 address.**

Seems like we need to ensure that we see the same well-known address only once and if we can see get the same IPv4 address with a different NAT64 prefix lengths then we should skip this and/or use with other well-known address.... I guess that may be why there are two well-kwon addresses :)

We did this in Node.js as a short-term "solution" since the `Construct` call modifies global state. The downside is that it'll result in deadlock if you make a nested `construct` call within the same provider.

I believe the Python SDK has the same problem as Node.js (modifying global state in `Construct`).

We’re going to need to find a better long-term solution. For Node.js, we’ll likely end up making use of [`vm.createContext`](https://nodejs.org/api/vm.html#vm_vm_createcontext_contextobject_options) to run the user's `construct` in its own context (with it's own globals). I'm planning to try this out this week.

Go doesn't have this problem as each `Construct` call will get its own [`pulumi.Context`](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/ee2f65510bd8129adffb18d7e198bf12c1b84fee/sdk/go/pulumi/context.go#L44).

The short-term thing for Python would be to do the same thing we did with Node.js (use locks to serialize `Construct` calls). That would at least ensure correctness when there are multiple concurrent `Construct` calls happening within the same process.

But I'm not sure what the right long-term solution will be for Python. Launch a new `python` process for each `Construct` call? Is there an equivalent of `vm.createContext` for Python?

It'd be helpful to break this method down since it's doing several different things and that makes it hard to reliably follow the details of the threading-related parts.  In particular it doesn't seem like it's necessary to obtain the reader lock if the reader already exists, because then you can just directly call `wake()/set()` to trigger an update.

FWIW, I'm also not entirely sure why it makes sense to trigger this logic from `Runs()` as well as from `__init__()`.  I guess because there's a chance the reader was not created due to a transient issue (the "no DebugEvent file set is found in the logdir") so we want to retry periodically?  But since this logic tightly couples both the reader creation and the immediate-update-triggering logic, it's not clear to me if `Runs()` also needs to trigger an immediate update of the reader (since the logic in `Runs()` doesn't actually use the reader directly, and it would be an asynchronous update anyway), or if that just sort of happens as an unnecessary side effect.

More comments about the expected lifecycle would help here, as would breaking this into more granular pieces that are explicitly called elsewhere in the class when needed.  For example:

```
def __init__(self):
    ...
    self._reader = None
    self._reader_lock = threading.Lock()
    self._monitors = []
    self._reload_needed_event = None
    self._tryCreateReader()
    self._tryReloadReader()

def _tryCreateReader(self):
    # Attempt to create the reader, using double-checked locking.
    if not self._reader:
        with self._reader_lock:
            if not self._reader:
                self._reader = self._createReader()
    # Reader creation may fail due to transient errors; abort and try later.
    if not self._reader:
        return
    self._monitors = self._createMonitors(self._reader)
    self._reload_needed_event = run_repeatedly_in_background(
        self._reader.update, DEFAULT_RELOAD_INTERVAL_SEC)

def _tryReloadReader(self)
    if self._reader:
        self._reload_needed_event.set()

def _createReader(self):
    ...

def _createMonitors(self, reader):
    ...
```

I am not 100% sure here, I think the point of this function is to make assertions about the overall structure of a trace rather than about a single span.

The support for `children=None` was my way of allowing someone to determine how deep they wanted to test (e.g. the first 2-3 levels of spans, rather than the entire trace).

By not providing a default you force people to make that decision, and I don't want people to get used to not supplying `children` as a way to test that there are no children, since `children=None` ignores children testing and `children=()` asserts that there are no children.

Maybe we need to rethink the API for this helper in general, maybe there is a more ergonomic way to make this type of assertion. This really was only my first pass through this anyways, wouldn't be surprised if we could come up with something better.

> I don't understand how changing this from **kwargs to kwargs keeps this a tiny, very scoped PR

Because all methods where `_client` is used, have a `kwargs` that may or may not have `agent`, `pool` and `persistent` defined.

So either: we rely on `_client` being an internal function alter those `kwargs` by `pop`-ing the keyword arguments that are not supposed to be passed further down the road, notice that that relies on the kwargs argument being the same dict than in each of the methods.
The alternative would be to clean the `kwargs` dict in each method before `request`, which is likely way too much work for this particular thing and that time would be better spent dealing with the issues I mentioned.

Your suggestion of an alternative `_client` signature is not enough, since it'd still mean `agent` and `pool` are passed to `treq.HttpClient.request`, triggering the warning/exception.

The "burden" I mention is because `treq.get`, `treq.put`, `treq.post`, `treq.HttpClient.get`, `treq.HttpClient.put`, ... all have pretty much the exact same interface, so any changes have to be done consistently in all those places.
The fact that everything ends in `treq.HttpClient.request` does mean less work and less errors.

> As to raising a warning, can't **kwargs to HTTPClient.request() and raise a warning if it's not empty? Then after 'awhile' just delete the **kwargs and the warning code. Then we don't have to keep maintaining this PR for a few releases.

I don't think v2 tests need changes at all:

1. The tensor kernel, e.g. `resize_image_tensor` only branches on `antialias` to bail out in case it is set together with an `interpolation` that doesn't support it: 

   https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2cd25c1a05012a3720a87f20cec436811fadeedd/torchvision/prototype/transforms/functional/_geometry.py#L150-L153

   Thus, our `sample_inputs_fn`'s, that should cover all _valid_ branches, don't set it at all:

   - https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2cd25c1a05012a3720a87f20cec436811fadeedd/test/prototype_transforms_kernel_infos.py#L265
   - https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2cd25c1a05012a3720a87f20cec436811fadeedd/test/prototype_transforms_kernel_infos.py#L275
   - https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2cd25c1a05012a3720a87f20cec436811fadeedd/test/prototype_transforms_kernel_infos.py#L277
2. Our reference test check the image tensor against the corresponding PIL kernel, e.g. `resize_image_pil`. Since PIL "hardcodes" `antialias=True`, we need to do the same:

   https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2cd25c1a05012a3720a87f20cec436811fadeedd/test/prototype_transforms_kernel_infos.py#L280-L286

   https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2cd25c1a05012a3720a87f20cec436811fadeedd/test/prototype_transforms_kernel_infos.py#L304-L309

   (I saw that I messed up the `antialias` value here. Will send a PR soon)
3. Since the dispatcher, e.g. `resize`, no longer contains any logic besides the dispatching, we just use the kernel inputs and check if they are dispatched to the correct kernel:

I thought about this as well and I thought about 3 solutions, which I think all aren't the greatest

- Using channels to send error messages to the user. Two reasons why I don't really like this idea: 
    1. It is unnecessarily complex and introduces a new dependency on django channels
    2. Messages are only received if the user is on the right page at the right time if a developer only implements the default pages. Although he could subscribe to the channel on every page of his application if he so chooses. In that case, this would bring the best end-user experience I think.

- Saving errors to the database and then we've got 2 ways to show these errors to the user
    1. Writing some kind of custom middleware to read these from the database and show messages if there are any (not a great solution, this means two extra db-calls for every page-load of every user -> one for retrieving and one for deleting)
    2. Only showing these error-messages on some pages used in the admin-dashboard. And if the developer wants to show these messages on other pages as well, he'll have to add the messages on these pages himself. (we could define some function show_upload_errors_as_messages(request))

Regarding the file location - I think that it should be predictable (i.e. fixed), so better to me is `~/.lexicon_tld_cache` instead of current directory. One reason to have this fixed is that the file is updated only when it is old (or missing), so it could be reused from multiple instances of `lexicon` running from different directories (but not concurrently, of course, because of risk of concurrent update).

There are some recommendations for application data storage (on Linux this is `/var/cache` or `~/.cache`, on Windows - ?, on iOS - ?), but I cannot find any library handling this, so easiest is to simply use `~/.lexicon_tld_cache` and let `expanduser` function place it in the correct home directory.

Alternative would be to always download the TLDs (not store it anywhere), but I prefer the above.

The `top_level_key` is used in the following scenario:

```python
foo = FooResource.configure_at_runtime()
bar = BarResource(inner=foo)

defs = Definitions(
    ...
    resources={
        "foo": foo,
        "bar": bar,
    }
)
```

Here, we need to affiliate the `inner` input to `bar` with the `foo`  top-level resource key, so we know how to populate the fully-configured resource at runtime.

The current approach is to:
- At Definitions time, associate all top-level resources’ `top_level_key` with the key they are given in the Definitions object
- If resource `A` depends on resource `B`, when we query `A`’s `required_resource_keys`, we look up `B’s` `top_level_key` and return it

An alternative which would remove statefulness is to
- At resource init time, generate a UUID (or perhaps a deterministic ID which remains the same if the repo does not change), `unique_id` for each resource.
- If resource `A` depends on resource `B`, when we query `A`’s `required_resource_keys`, we look up `B`’s `unique_id`. Then, we look at each of the top-level resources in the repo and find the resource key associated with that `unique_id`.

Here the unique_id is set when the resource is constructed and its only purpose is to uniquely identify a resource when figuring out the associated top-level key.

What I would consider ideal is that -- and let's set aside the details of how the parsing happens -- both `globus transfer` and `globus timer create transfer` use the same parsing methodology and same parse function, potentially parametrized for their different cases.

I'd like to see the `globus transfer` case implemented as:
```python
if batch:
    tdata = magical_parse_batch_input(batch)
else: ...
```
and the `globus timer create transfer` case implemented as:
```python
if batch:
    tdata = magical_parse_batch_input(batch, external_checksum_supported=False)
else:
    ...
```

That way, we don't have two different overall parse behaviors, and it's possible to formulate a single input which is valid for both. Even if we have an unfortunate situation like `--external-checksum` in which some inputs are valid for one but not the other, it makes it possible for this to work some of the time.

In principle, I would be fine with `magical_parse_batch_input` doing CSV, shlexed line-reads, newline-delimited JSON, any other "reasonable" format, or even detection based on the first line of input (imagine trying different parses until one works). In practice, I don't know that we want to bother about having multiple ways of parsing those inputs today.

Even then, `tools` would limit subprocesses to only binaries that you can find on `PATH`, which is not ideal. This is a very good argument in favour of allowing runnable dependencies (only once we have proper scoped dependency support)

So just to check, you're suggesting we make both the setters and getters access HuffmanCode (not a pointer):

```
/* Getters and setters must be given a HuffmanCode. */        
#define BROTLI_HC_GET_BITS(H) (H.bits)                        
#define BROTLI_HC_GET_VALUE(H) (H.value)
```

(and with FAST_LOAD:)

```
/* Getters and setters must be given a HuffmanCode. */   
#define BROTLI_HC_GET_BITS(H) (H & 0xFFu)                
#define BROTLI_HC_GET_VALUE(H) (H >> 16)
```

And then we drop the PREPARE_TO_ACCESS and UPDATE macros, and just do things like:

```
static BROTLI_INLINE uint32_t DecodeSymbol(uint32_t bits,                  
                                           const HuffmanCode* table,       
                                           BrotliBitReader* br) {          
  HuffmanCode huffman_code;                                                
  table += bits & HUFFMAN_TABLE_MASK;                                      
  huffman_code = *table;                                                   
  if (BROTLI_HC_GET_BITS(huffman_code) > HUFFMAN_TABLE_BITS) {             
    uint32_t nbits = BROTLI_HC_GET_BITS(huffman_code) - HUFFMAN_TABLE_BITS;
    BrotliDropBits(br, HUFFMAN_TABLE_BITS);                                
    table += BROTLI_HC_GET_VALUE(huffman_code);                            
    table += (bits >> HUFFMAN_TABLE_BITS) & BitMask(nbits);                
    huffman_code = *table;                                                 
  }                                                                        
  BrotliDropBits(br, BROTLI_HC_GET_BITS(huffman_code));                    
  return BROTLI_HC_GET_VALUE(huffman_code);                                
}
```

I think that the problem here is related to a more general issue - whether the object of class should be fully initialised after a call to `__init__`.

The best approach is to first set all atributes using default (reasonable) values and later update them to a custom ones. Setting kernel to a default (not-None) value is one of possibilities, but I'd do it without specifying explicitely what kind of projectile it is. I'd rather use following code:

```
projectile = Projectile()
kernel = KernelModel(projectile)
field = Field(kernel)
```

or even better:
```
field=Field()
```
This require implementation of respective `__init__` methods which when no argument is provided, would use some reasonble defaults (i.e. 'H' as a projectile name).


Take a look at `_field_args` https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/master/pytrip/tripexecuter/execparser.py#L92 and `_parse_extra_args` and `_update_obj` methods. 

Parsing of projectile argument would now add an attribute called `projectile` to the `self.plan.fields[-1]` object (if I am correct). 

What do you think of using a different method that a `_update_obj` for a "projectile" key in `_field_args` ?

Such method (i.e. called `_update_kernel_obj`) could look like:
```
def _update_kernel_obj(_obj, _objattr, _val, _format):
    _update_obj(_obj.kernel, _objattr, _val, _format)
```

The most important question of this PR is whether we care about supporting the edge cases of a `Snapshot` or a target's `sources` field having files with different source roots?

When might this happen? For targets, when you have a [target aggregate](https://www.pantsbuild.org/target_aggregate.html). For filesystem specs, when you use a very loose glob like `./src python test '**/*.py'`.

--

Conceptually, it is the "correct" thing to do to support this use case. The point of these rules are to strip all source roots, regardless of whether the source roots look like their neighbors.

Concretely, without this feature, if a user ever had this case where they tried mixing files with different source roots, then the engine would fail because it would try, for example, stripping `src/python` from the file `tests/python/pants_test/example.py`.

--

Why not do this? It makes the implementation more complex and almost certainly less performant. 

If we didn't care about this edge case, then we would change the `SnapshotToStrip` class to have an additional field called something like `example_file`. We would determine one single prefix to strip for the entire collection, exclusively determined by the `example_file`. For targets, `example_file` would be the target address. For snapshots, it'd be a random file, likely.

Compared to this simpler implementation (which is what we had before this PR), this PR's implementation has a few performance implications:

* We call `SourceRoots.find_by_path` on _every_ source file used transitively for goals like `test`, compared to calling it once per every target used transitively.
* We must call `groupby` on every single source file used transitively.
* We must call `SnapshotSubset` and `DirectoriesToMerge`.

I haven't profiled, but things _felt_ slower with this PR and the source root stripping showed up in `--v2-ui`.

--

If we decide we do care about supporting this edge case, then we can possibly make these optimizations:

1. If there is only one source root for the `Snapshot`, then early return without needing to use `SnapshotSubset` and `DirectoriesToMerge`.
1. Memoize `SourcesRoot.find_by_path`?

(As an aside, I've just noticed that currently we're wrapping `$executable` in quotes when we execute it, which is actually incorrect, and means we won't correctly operate on things like aliases and things with quotes and escaped characters. This is a good idea, but in this particular case, this won't give us any benefit.

numpy array will not fit us to store Series and DataFrame objects, because they implement `__array_interface__` and so numpy will grab their data as a pure array, without preserving metadata such as names:
```python
>>> np.array([pandas.Series([1, 2, 3])], dtype=object)
array([[1, 2, 3]], dtype=object)
```

So we want to have `by` as a list inside of `groupby_agg_builder`, and we also want to [concatenate](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/5a6c93dad9a658c8f7a09eed020ceba5df7b41a8/modin/backends/pandas/query_compiler.py#L2641) `by` list with `not_broadcastable_by`, so it also have to be a python list.

So in case of using mask instead of 2 for loops via 'by' we will get 3:
1. iterate trough `by` to create a mask
2. create a numpy array from `by`, to be able to grab elements from it via mask
3. convert `not_broadcastable_by` back to list again, to be able to append it at `by` inside `groupby_agg_builder`

The following is more efficient and has lower memory use at cost of being more verbose. The key component is using the `sparse` argument to meshgrid which gives much smaller broadcastable coordinate arrays.


```Python
    center = (size / 2) - 0.5
    dist = 0
    for g in np.meshgrid(*L, sparse=True):
        g -= center
        dist = dist + g * g
    dist = np.sqrt(dist)
    coords = np.zeros((ndim,) + dist.shape, dtype=np.double)
    coords[0] = dist + center
```

It may also be sufficient to just use `np.float32` for the `coords` array.

~~~ python
>>> default_config = DefaultAttrDict(IntegrationConfig)
>>> default_config.some_integration
IntegrationConfig("some_integration")
~~~
This is based on the fact that `__getattr__` is called _conditionally_ when the attribute is not actually present on the instance (`default_config` in the example below). In this case we first try bubbling up the request for the attribute to the superclass(es). If we still can't find an attribute, we create one using the constructor we passed to `DefaultAttrDict` (`IntegrationConfig` in this case, which takes two arguments), store it as an item (which can be accessed as an attribute because of the super `AttrDict`), and return that. Subsequent attempts to access the same attribute (`some_integration` in this example), will return the instance of `IntegrationConfig` created on the first access.

This is a mock.Mock() object mocking an OCSP response object. The OCSP object it is mocking has the this and next update times in datetime objects in UTC, but with tzinfo=None. The original code was using datetime.now(), which actually wasn't quite right. This has the right format (i.e. tzinfo=None), but returns local time, not UTC.

pytz.UTC.localize() does the exact opposite of what is needed here, i.e. it adds tzinfo to a datetime object where it is None:

>>> time = datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 18, 12, 0)
>>> time
datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 18, 12, 0)
>>> pytz.UTC.localize(time)
datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 18, 12, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)

We want to remove the tzinfo, not add:

>>> now = datetime.datetime.now(pytz.UTC)
>>> now
datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 17, 23, 9, 16, 174051, tzinfo=<UTC>)
>>> now.replace(tzinfo=None)
datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 17, 23, 9, 16, 174051)

datetime.UTC.fromutc() does not do the right thing either. It is a no-op when the tzinfo already is pytz.UTC:

>>> now = datetime.datetime.now(pytz.UTC)
>>> now
datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 17, 23, 9, 16, 174051, tzinfo=<UTC>)
>>> pytz.UTC.fromutc(now)
datetime.datetime(2023, 7, 17, 23, 9, 16, 174051, tzinfo=<UTC>)

Hrm, the `listVisitedPages` method updates the returned value of the computed `this.visitedPages` property - this is different to setting `this.currentLocation` which is data, and so is local state that is being updated.

This means that when you then call `this.updateContentState()` the updates you have made to `this.visitedPages` will only be present because Vue has cached the output of the `visitedPages` computed property that you have then modified. If the computed property were to be recomputed for some reason, then your modifications would be lost.

Two possible solutions:

Do what currentLocation and savedLocation do - have one in data, and one that is a computed prop derived from extraFields.contentState.

Pass the `currentLocation` to `updatedContentState` and just update `visitedPages` on the `contentState`, and then rely on that being set into the `extraFields` prop from outside the component, to then update the computed prop `visitedPages`

We pass `AfdCertificateType.managed_certificate` as parameter, which implicitly convert it as string, but `Enum`'s `__str__` method changes in 3.11:
``` python
from enum import Enum

class Foo(str, Enum):
    BAR = "bar"

# Python 3.10
f"{Foo.BAR}"  # > bar

# Python 3.11
f"{Foo.BAR}"  # > Foo.BAR
```
`__str__` returns the classname.member (AfdCertificateType.MANAGED_CERTIFICATE) rather than member's value.
Add `.value` to get the real value of Enum.

> Does attaching to only the main thread pause all the child threads as well?

Yes, in FreeBSD, `PT_ATTACH` sends `SIGSTOP` to the whole process:
https://github.com/freebsd/freebsd/blob/7f19ffe6f947e40299a4be93b7cf320da2e17951/sys/kern/sys_process.c#L955


I test this behavior indirectly here:
https://github.com/benfred/py-spy/blob/d79a69a4f6f380a179944106126ce88265c4bab1/remoteprocess/src/freebsd/mod.rs#L254-L261

In  contrary, Linux `ptrace` operates solely on threads:

>  Therefore,  "tracee"  always  means  "(one)  thread", never "a (possibly multithreaded) process".  Ptrace commands are always sent to a specific tracee using  a  call  of  the form

So, BSD ptrace gives us more flexibility here.

Because it was also drawn onto an image that had the same WCS.  So the first time, we built it directly in image coordinates and drew it onto an image with just a pixel_scale WCS.  

The second time, we tell it to project onto sky coordinates according to some WCS.  But we draw it onto an image with that same WCS.  So we should get the same thing in the end as the first case, but this time it round tripped through sky coordinates.

The point is that the PSF really does always look the same on the SCA, regardless of where the sky is.  So if we want to have a PSF in sky coordinates (as we normally do), then we need to tell it the WCS we will eventually draw onto so that it can project to the sky correctly, and then drawImage will project it back.

While it clones the contents of the generator, the generator is still considered "drained" after the first pass. Since we have code after the `yield`, that code only gets fired once, instead of firing after each plugin has a chance to iterate over the ready states. Reading the PEB to get the command line parameters still seem to be the best method if somebody has tampered with the parameters after creating the process.
For instance, create a process as suspended, patch the command line in its PEB and unfreeze it.
The process will use the "new" parameters whereas the system (with `NtQueryInformationProcess`) will give you the "old" ones (see here : https://blog.xpnsec.com/how-to-argue-like-cobalt-strike/)
By default, reading from PEB is still allowed if one has sufficient privileges except for protected processes where one must use `NtQueryInformationProcess`.
So the PEB method will work for almost all processes on windows 8.1+ except the few (4-5) protected processes lying around (where we can fallback on NtQIP), and it will accurately report the actual command line parameters used

Passing the flag `--include-java` to `ray start` command on every machine is dangerous!
We must make sure that the value of this flag on each machine is the same.

If `Node1`'s `--include-java` is true, and `Node2`s' is false, when user run a Java driver on `Node1`, the Java task may forward to `Node2`, and `Node2` will fail to start a Java worker because of the incorrect java worker command.

I think the best practice for this is to specify this flag for head node, and all worker nodes follow the head.

I don't really like this. The individual simplify methods are popping from a list that we're iterating over, that's never a good idea. 

Even if we're iterating over a copy here, that might actually cause even more issues, as the `enumeration` that these functions receive is simply the index number on that copy, however the moment we pop out any value, all of the indices change. 

Say we're at index 5 that's popped, now we go to index 6 in a copy, but in the original, where we popped from, index 5 was removed, and now became index 6, which means index 6 is now actually index 7, etc. Its' just really messy.

Instead, I'd go for an appraoch that simply returns the indices to be removed, like this:
```python
    def _simplify_doubled_items(self) -> set[int]:
        """Get indices of all doubled items that can be removed."""
        to_remove = set()

        for index, item in enumerate(self.parsed):
            try:
                next_item = self.parsed[index + 1]
            except IndexError:  # Last item (without any next item)
                break

            if item == next_item:
                to_remove.add(index)

        return to_remove
        
    def _simplify_doubled_colors(self) -> set[int]:
        """Get indices of all doubled color items.

        As colors (obviously) override each other, we only ever care about the last one, ignore
        the previous ones. (for example: specifying red color, then orange, then yellow, then some text
        will just result in yellow text)
        """
        to_remove = set()

        prev_color = None
        for index, item in enumerate(self.parsed):
            if isinstance(item, (MinecraftColor, WebColor)):
                # If we found a color after another, remove the previous color
                if prev_color is not None:
                    to_remove.add(prev_color)
                prev_color = index

            # If we find a string, that's what our color we found previously applies to,
            # set prev_color to None, marking this color as used
            if isinstance(item, str):
                prev_color = None

        return to_remove
```

Which can then simply be unioned together like
```python
def simplify(self) -> Self:
    to_remove = set()
    to_remove.union(self._simplify_doubled_colors())
    to_remove.union(self._simplify_doubled_items())
    
    parsed = self.parsed.copy()

    # To safely remove multiple indices by popping, we need to go from last index
    # in a sorted order, so that the indices don't change on us.
    for index in sorted(to_remove, reverse=True):
        parsed.pop(index)
    
    return __class__(parsed)
```

Notice that in this example, the individual simpify methods actually belong to `Motd` class itself. While this does introduce some clutter in that class, it's much less weird than having a class like `MotdSimplifiers` that's basically just a namespace for a bunch of simplification methods.

You basically changed the semantics of the `start_kinesis` function such that it also waits for the server to be up.
But this also means that you don't perform the check if the server has already been created (`account_id` in `_server`).
This is very dangerous, since this means that the following case won't work:
- Send a request to kinesis, which triggers the startup of the service and the backend server.
- After a few milliseconds (the server has been created but is not up yet), send another request.
  - This request will be forwarded to the backend even though it's not up yet.

So there are basically only two options:
- Avoid changing the semantics of `start_server` and just start the server asynchronously and don't wait for it to be up. Perform the check on every request (in the forwarder within the provider), as it was done before.
- Perform the check here, but implement a proper locking mechanism.

In both cases we could remove the `asynchronous` parameter though.

> If python is not running on the same machine as TM1, we shouldn't even worry about the number of threads.

Thanks for your thoughts and feedback @rkvinoth. I think I disagree. As TM1 admins or developers, I think we do care about not overloading the TM1 server with work.
I mean when TM1py sends those CellPut statements to the TM1 Server, the heavy lifting is done by TM1. Python is actually just waiting for a response from the server most of the time. TM1 is digesting the values and updating the cube structures. 
On the client-side python can use 4 or 10 threads even if you have just one free CPU. That's not problematic because most of the (python) threads will be waiting most of the time anyways for TM1 to finish digesting the API requests.
 
On the server-side, I would expect to see (in Arc, Pulse or tm1top) 4 or 10 threads processing almost at every moment.
So on the server side, we are causing TM1 to be busy. If the server has let's say 16 CPUs, I would argue we shouldn't use all of them for writing because then if TM1 users open views at this moment there are limited CPU resources to cater for their session and they may experience delays.

The more complex solution might involve the server actively prioritizing which items to sync, in which case having a separate 'ordering' field may be better, so that the server could decide to queue up one sync before another.

Another alternative would be to use the datetime field as the primary ordering, could then also have a simple 'priority' flag to bump some syncs ahead of the other.

yeah, it's a little confusing, but it returns an awaitable object. This has to do with the fact that we're returning a context manager that both has an `__await__` and a `__aenter__` etc. So this way, you can call it like this:
```python
response = await client.send_request(request)
```
and like this (mainly in the case of streaming)
```python
async with client.send_request(request) as response:
    print(response)
```

talked through this with @johanste and he's ok with not making it `async def`. Reason is once we mark it as `async def`, the compiler thinks we're returning a coroutine, which don't have attributes `__aenter__`, so we get an attribute error when trying to use it as a context managed response

> Do you know if flake8 is going to be run on the diff, or on entire folders?

By default, it will run on any file that was changed or added in the commit. Each hook knows which files it can handle so if you just add python files, the checks for yaml and toml will be skipped. 

> Also I'm wondering whether this may actually get in our way, rather than help: some devs have workflows where they commit or amend often with very messy code that would not pass the checks, and only publish when they have a decent version.

The obvious way out is to simply not use `pre-commit` hooks if you have a different workflow. You can still `pre-commit run` manually to check the current diff. So, IMO we should keep it. Otherwise, contributors need to run multiple commands to get all formatting checks, which is worse.

This is a case where the type checking gets a little confusing. A SecretBundle can have a `None` `value`, which is why this `Optional[str]` type makes sense -- but also, [SecretSetParameters](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/main/sdk/keyvault/azure-keyvault-secrets/azure/keyvault/secrets/_generated/v7_2/models/_models_py3.py#L560) requires a non-`None` value. We have to send over SecretSetParameters to create a secret in the first place, meaning that -- in theory -- any secret we get back and turn into a KeyVaultSecret _should_ have a value for `value`. `with` is slightly more complicated to achieve in one pass so I've split this into it's own function.

Basically the different variations of bracketed with statements give pretty different parse trees so we need to recursively solve this.

`with (open("test.txt")) as f: ...`: this is an `asexpr_test`
`with (open("test.txt") as f): ...`: this is an `atom` containing an `asexpr_test`
`with (open("test.txt")) as f, (open("test.txt")) as f: ...`: this is `asexpr_test`, `COMMA`, `asexpr_test`
`with (open("test.txt") as f, open("test.txt")) as f: ...`: this is an `atom` containing a `testlist_gexp` which then contains multiple `asexpr_test`s

Are you trying to prevent `add_subdirectory` from changing the global variables? The problem is a "bug" in `3rdparty/onnx-tensorrt/` (ie. they shouldn't change the global variables in the first place). A better workaround compared to manually saving `BUILD_SHARED_LIBS` is to call `add_subdirectory` inside a function. Each function in cmake has it's own scope, and `add_subdirectory` can then only modify the variables inside the function scope.

I think we are mixing point of views :)
You are speaking from the patroni side, how it sees the cluster, while I am from the postgresql side.
> Nope. The this topology example is incorrect. Absence of leader doesn't make any other node a new leader automatically., therefore there should be a `+ ` before postgresql3.

It's taken from the real cluster, where I've emulated connection issue between patroni on leader and dcs with large enough retry_timeout. While leader is retrying and postgresql is alive on it, during retry timeout there will be a situation, when leader key will be missing from dcs. So from the patroni side, this cluster do not have a leader. But, if we look into postgres, there obviously will be one master (`postgresql3`) and replica (`postgresql4`), as it's shown on topology. 

Moreover, the absence of leader key in such situations makes building topology graph very complicated, as there will be no vertex, from which we can start traversing it.

I understand that scenario like this with dcs failures are rare enough, but I think that presenting a true topology in such cases is very important in order to make decisions on real state of things.

Both of these approaches seem to be more less efficient in terms of execution time compared to declaring an additional dict outside the converter function if this converter function need to be called repeatedly.

Agree that `OT_ERROR_PARSE` is not a proper error code. The DNS response code for domain not matching is `kResponseNotZone` and `kResponseNotAuth`:
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/c03458433ec188be21027d6fa0eab01d3dbf6c03/src/core/net/dns_headers.hpp#L267-L268

But we currently don't have an OT error code that is mapping to `kResponseNotZone`. For now, I think `OT_ERROR_SECURITY` may be a better error code as it will be converted to `kResponseRefused`:
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/c03458433ec188be21027d6fa0eab01d3dbf6c03/src/core/net/srp_server.cpp#L71-L74
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/c03458433ec188be21027d6fa0eab01d3dbf6c03/src/core/net/dns_headers.hpp#L263

One thing to notice is that the SRP client converts `kResponseNotZone` to `OT_ERROR_PARSE`, which I think is not perfect either.
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/c03458433ec188be21027d6fa0eab01d3dbf6c03/include/openthread/srp_client.h#L173

For the server, one solution is that we can have those DNS processing method return `Dns::Response` but not OT error code. I initially considered adding such an `assert` (also adding an `assert` check to ensure entry is not already freed), but decided against adding them:
- Main reason is to avoid the extra overhead (in term of code size and run-time checks) that such asserts would add (in all usages of these methods), particularly since the template methods are typically inlined, so these extra checks would be included everywhere.
- I feel incorrect use of the `Pool` methods would be rather obvious error (and therefore unlikely).
   - In most use-cases, we would simply have a pool of objects that we just allocate entries from, and then later free, and incorrect use of the `Pool` APIs would be almost not possible (or a very obvious coding error).
   - If there are more complex usage where we may benefit from the extra `assert` checks, I think better approach would be to leave it to the users of the `Pool` method to add it (e.g., we can define a subclass of a specific `Pool<Type>` and re-define `Free()` in it to add any extra checks/asserts, and this would apply to that sub-class type).
- Also the existing implementations (which are being replaced by `Pool` class methods) do not include such `assert` checks.

> For encoding several tiles with different behaviour, a simple concatenation is sufficient:

did you mean "layer" here instead of tile? Just to make sure I'm following you, we could achieve per layer differences in a single tile by encoding each separately as their own tile first and concatenating the payloads together to merge into a single tile, due to how protobufs work?

> The problem is that when decoding the "sum" of these two elements (with potentially several extents, y_coord_down, etc), I will need to give a dictionary (indexed by layer_name) to apply different options per layer.

Good call out, I  neglected to consider the decode case, which doesn't offer any option per layer. I was assuming for encode that callers would effectively perform the same operations as in the top level [encode](https://github.com/tilezen/mapbox-vector-tile/blob/2dfeb11db9cc40165a14b32c6285ddf0357b3dcb/mapbox_vector_tile/__init__.py#L18-L24) function but with different options as necessary when adding each layer.

Whatever option we choose, it's important for it to be symmetric between encode/decode. I can think of 3 possible options, which may not all be viable:

1. allow options to specify differences per layer to support encoding/decoding through a single call. Encode/decode options should be the same unless there's a good reason or them to diverge.
2. For per layer differences, callers add the layers one at a time, and extract the layers one at a time as well by name. We'd likely need to also grow a call to expose the list of layer names beforehand to support decoding.
3. If tiles can be generated by concatenating separate layer payloads, can we rely on the same mechanism to extract them out this way as well? Similar to 2, I assume we'd also need to provide a way to surface the ordering of layer names.

My preference would be option 1, since my expectation is that this would keep the call site code simplest. However, I think we'd need to think through supporting various scenarios, eg I think it'd be important for the per layer differences to be thought of as overrides, otherwise it'd be unclear how to decode layers that weren't specified. We could default to whatever we do now, but it might make more sense for callers to specify that fallback when there are per layer differences.

Maybe that implies that we leave the options the way they are now, and there's a separate catch all  to configure any option more specifically for each layer? I expect per layer options to be the minority case, so keeping the caller complexity isolated to that case seems justified. eg`decode(..., y_coord_down=True, per_layer=dict(layer_foo=dict(y_coord_down=False))`

Change of plan (after talking on Slack): if any user-provided steps (i.e. "chunks") go out of bounds for any TTree, it raises an error.

This strictness has a benefit—if we know that we'll get the entry ranges we ask for or die trying, the Dask array `chunks` or dask-awkward `divisions` can be known before `.compute()`. As a consequence, there will be fewer `unknown_lengths` in type-tracer arrays, so unknown values propagate less, and downstream situations that need known lengths or values won't have to give up. It may be that some workflow is impossible with `open_files=False` and no steps in `files`, but possible with them (which is a nice incentive).

It also has no way to determine which messages failed or succeeded. ```python
AVAILABLE_DECOMPRESSORS = {
    "brotli": brotli.decompress,

}

def decompress_body(body, encoding):

In the last PR, there was some clean up to ensure that we were not using the registered task object as a transitory state object during task registration. This PR has reintroduced some of that concerning use again.

As a general rule, the validator functions should be pure functions without side effects - if we need to set the `extra_metadata` property - it seems that returning `extra_metadata` as a key on the dict that gets returned from the validator function is the appropriate way to do this, not by assigning it to the global registered task object.

Again, the issue here is if two tasks are being enqueued at the same time, with shared memory at play, then the use of the global object could lead either to the extra_metadata for one queuing being applied to another, or the extra metadata for one being cleared while another is being queued. It's a recipe for hard to narrow down bugs.

If I understand this correctly, this method will now use mutations for all insert statements, and DML for all other update statements (`UPDATE`, `DELETE`). I think we should try to make this in a slightly different way, so users can have some more control over it, and it can be used for update and delete as well. I would suggest:
1. Use mutations by default when the connection is in auto-commit mode. Reason: Mutations do not support read-your-writes, but that is not possible in auto-commit mode anyways.
2. Use DML by default when the connection is in transactional mode (for the same reason as above).
3. Let the user configure whether a specific transaction should use DML or mutations. This could be through a custom method or a custom SQL statement. As far as I know, dbapi does not have a standard method for setting the isolation level for transactions. That is the method that we [used for ActiveRecord](https://github.com/googleapis/ruby-spanner-activerecord/blob/b083a8750babc2653fd4dc3b2ffa7ced47bf41c6/examples/snippets/mutations/application.rb#L19).
4. Also let the user turn off the use of mutations in auto-commit mode. Dbapi only accepts SQL strings as input, and this implementation tries to 'parse' the SQL string and convert it to a mutation in a relatively simple way. If the SQL string contains special characters, comments, etc. this parsing would currently fail.

Reasons for this are:
1. `assert`s are actually skipped when python is run with the `-O` optimize flag, and we can't guarantee nobody is doing that, so we should go out of our way to still provide the same sanity checking for those situations.

2. except where specific, standard exceptions are better (e.g. `KeyError`, `ValueError`), PGPy strives to always raise `PGPError` derived exceptions, to make it more straightforward to isolate recovery or bail-out conditions relating to PGP operations.

- IO loops are non re-entrant, so you cannot block on a coroutine if the loop is already running
- If you create a new loop for those tasks it will have its own separate list of IO descriptors. So if it happens to await for anything that is managed by the original loop - it will block forever.

We can observe that the multiple-choice prompts always (1) print the choices in a specific way (ordered list with tabs and `-`) (2) using specific inputs (a numbered list). Having to read and write this every time and ensuring it's consistent is too much cognitive complexity.

Instead we can refactor into something like this:

```python
def prompt_choices(choices: List[str]) -> int:
    """Prompt choice and return its index."""
    for i, choice in enumerate(choices):
        click.echo(f"\t{i + 1} - {choice}")
    inputs = [str(i + 1) for i in range(len(choices))]
    input = click.prompt("Choice", type=click.Choice(inputs), show_choices=False)
    return int(input) - 1
```

This way,

```python
    click.echo("\t1 - AWS Quick Start Pipeline Templates\n\t2 - Custom Pipeline Template Location")

        _generate_from_custom_location()
    else:
        _generate_from_app_pipeline_templates()
```

This scenarios does indeed succeed, because the Patroni instances are still running. It is only the Postgres processes which are not starting due to the static_primary config (which is intended behavior).

Once that static_primary config is set to null in the DCS, then the Postgres replicas are able to start.

I think this is a point of confusion based on one of your recent comments in ha.py. The replica Patroni instances are not intended to sys.exit. The idea is to just keep Postgres from starting, and the Patroni process stays alive and will continue to check the DCS values periodically based on its control loop.

This will allow for a more flexible (and still safe) operator experience. Basically, cluster operators do not need to synchronize the steps of removing static_primary config and adding of new replicas. The operations are safe to overlap (fewer footguns).
